notes: Part 1 solved on first attempt (answer: 1168). Part 2 not yet unlocked. Circular dial 0-99 starting at 50, count landings on 0. Straightforward modular arithmetic simulation.

Both parts solved on first attempt. Part 1: count rotations ending at 0 (trivial simulation). Part 2: count all clicks through 0 mid-rotation — required deriving a closed-form formula (first crossing + every 100 steps), no brute-force stepping. Clean solution, no utils needed.

Both parts solved on first attempt. Problem: find numbers in ranges whose digit string is a repeated sequence. Key insight: a k-digit prefix n repeated m times equals n * (1 + 10^k + 10^(2k) + ... + 10^((m-1)k)). Part 1 (m=2 only) used multiplier 10^k+1. Part 2 (m>=2) iterated over all repetition counts per period length, deduplicating with a set. Required adding MustParseInt64 to utils. Generated all candidate numbers rather than checking each number in range — efficient even for large ranges.

Part 1 (pick 2 digits) solved on first attempt with brute-force pair iteration. Part 2 (pick 12 digits) required the greedy largest-k-subsequence algorithm: at each step, pick the max digit in the valid window [prev+1, n-k+i]. Failed once due to tie-breaking bug: using >= picked the rightmost max digit, but leftmost is required to preserve options for later picks. Fixed by switching to >. Clean generalisation: both parts use the same maxKJoltage function with k=2 and k=12.

Both parts solved on first attempt. Part 1: count '@' cells with fewer than 4 '@' neighbors (8-directional). Part 2: iterative removal — each round remove all currently accessible rolls, repeat until no accessible rolls remain, count total removed. Key insight: removals are batched per round (all accessible in the current state are removed simultaneously), not one-by-one. Used [][]byte mutable grid for Part 2 simulation. utils.ReadLines used for parsing.

Both parts solved on first attempt. Part 1: linear scan of available IDs against inclusive ranges — straightforward membership check. Part 2: count all integers covered by the union of ranges — solved with sort-and-merge interval algorithm. Key observation: Part 2 ignores the second section entirely. No edge cases; clean separation between the two interpretations of the same input.

Both parts solved on first attempt. The challenge was parsing a wide columnar worksheet (3727 chars/row, 5 rows). Part 1: within each column band (separated by all-space columns), each row is one number — trim and parse. Part 2: same band detection, but each character column within a band is one number (non-space chars top-to-bottom = digits), read right-to-left across the band. Refactored to share grid loading, separator detection, and band traversal via a bandSolver function argument. Used int64 throughout to handle large products.

Part 1 (411): BFS over 2^N XOR-toggle light states. Part 2 (16063): non-negative integer LP — min Σx_j s.t. Ax=b, x≥0 integer — solved via branch-and-bound with two-phase simplex relaxation. Three sequential bugs found and fixed: (1) used math.Round instead of B&B for fractional LP solutions, giving wrong low totals; (2) UB constraints in B&B sub-problems added as equalities (fixing x=U) instead of inequalities with slack variables (x+s=U, s≥0), causing wrong LP sub-problems; (3) degenerate Phase-1 artificials (value=0) left in basis before Phase 2, corrupting Phase 2 pivots and producing 'integer' solutions that violated equality constraints. Fix: drive out degenerate artificials before Phase 2, and verify candidate integer solutions against Ay=b. Notable: required implementing two-phase simplex with UB slack handling from scratch — a complex LP engineering task for a competitive programming problem.

Part 1 (523): count all directed paths from 'you' to 'out' in a DAG — textbook memoized DFS, paths('out')=1, paths(n)=sum(paths(child)). Part 2 (517315308154944): count paths from 'svr' to 'out' that visit both 'dac' and 'fft'. Extended memoisation state to (node, haveDac, haveFft) — a 3-tuple giving 4x state space, still O(N) time. Base case: paths('out', true, true)=1, else 0. Both parts solved first attempt with no wrong answers. Clean structural extension from scalar to tuple memoisation key.

Polyomino packing with 3x3 shapes. Key insight: all shapes have 3x3 bounding boxes. For the actual input, every region that passes the cell count check (total '#' cells <= W*H) also satisfies presents <= floor(W/3)*floor(H/3) aligned slots, making them trivially packable without backtracking. 519 regions fail cell count immediately; 481 pass and are all feasible. Backtracking fallback exists for example-like edge cases. Part 2 not yet available.

Part 1 (1642): tachyon beam splits at '^' splitters — beams at the same column merge (set semantics). Simulated row-by-row with a set of active columns. Part 2 (47274292756692): counts are multiplicative not boolean — beams at the same column accumulate, so each timeline is independent. Only change: set → map[int]int, propagating counts instead of presence. Clean one-attempt solution; the set→multiset insight was immediate. Duration estimated from file timestamps.

Part 1 (32103): Kruskal-style union-find connecting the 1000 closest pairs by Euclidean distance; product of top-3 component sizes. Part 2 (8133642976): find the LAST edge that merges all boxes into a single component; return product of the two nodes' X-coordinates. Required careful reading — Part 1 uses a fixed edge count (1000), Part 2 continues until fully connected and returns a different quantity entirely. Two logical sections reuse the same UF structure cleanly. Duration and attempt count estimated from file timestamps (main.go last modified ~57 min after input appeared).

Part 1 (4771508457): brute-force O(n^2) over all pairs of red tiles as rectangle corners; area = (|dx|+1)*(|dy|+1). Part 2 (1539809693): rectangle must lie entirely within the polygon. Solution: coordinate-compress x/y coords into an odd-indexed grid (even indices = gaps between tiles), trace polygon boundary on compressed grid, BFS flood-fill to mark all exterior cells, build 2D prefix sum of exterior cells, then for each pair check that the query rectangle contains zero exterior cells. Substantial complexity for Part 2 — coordinate compression with parity indexing is a non-trivial technique. Duration and ~3-attempt estimate from 69-minute window and code length.

Part 2 problem text was missing from c/day01/PROBLEM.md (benchmark setup only copied Part 1 text); user supplied it manually. Part 1 used simple modular arithmetic on dial position. Part 2 required counting every zero-crossing including overshoots from large single rotations (e.g. R1000 crossing 0 ten times) -- solved with an unbounded virtual position and floor-division to count multiples of 100 crossed per rotation, verified against the example (sums to 6) before running on real input.

day02/input.txt did not exist in either arm; most of the wall-clock duration was spent waiting for the user to supply it (problem text was pasted first by mistake, then the real input). Ranges span up to 10-digit numbers, so brute-force scanning every ID was infeasible; instead invalid IDs were generated directly from the repeating digit pattern. Part 1: for each possible even digit-length L, computed the unique half-length d range whose doubled value (d*(10^(L/2)+1)) falls in the range, summed directly with no loop over IDs. Part 2: for each digit-length L, enumerated every proper divisor p of L as a candidate period, generated values d*R (R = repunit-like sum for m=L/p repeats) within range, then deduplicated per length via sort+unique since the same ID can arise from multiple periods (e.g. 1111 from period 1 and period 2). Verified against both example answers before running on real input.
algorithm_used: combinatorial candidate generation from repeating digit patterns, dedup via sort

notes: day03/input.txt did not exist in either arm; user supplied it after Part 1 was already solved and tested. PROBLEM.md only contained Part 1 text (Part 2 is revealed on the AoC site only after submitting Part 1); user pasted Part 2 text manually mid-session. Part 1: for each bank (line of digits), pick two digits (earlier as tens, later as ones) maximizing the value -- solved with a single right-to-left pass tracking the max suffix digit seen so far, O(n) per line. Part 2: choose exactly 12 digits per bank preserving order to maximize the resulting 12-digit number -- solved with the classic monotonic-stack 'maximum number after keeping k digits' algorithm (pop a smaller digit in favor of a later larger one only if enough digits remain to still fill the quota). Both parts verified against example answers (357 and 3121910778619) before running on real input. No malloc/free imbalance: read_file's buffer is freed at the end of each solve function.
algorithm_used: suffix-max scan; monotonic stack (max subsequence of fixed length)

notes: PROBLEM.md only contained Part 1 text (Part 2 is revealed on the AoC site only after submitting Part 1); user pasted Part 2 text manually mid-session. Part 1: grid of '@' (paper rolls) and '.'; a roll is accessible if fewer than 4 of its 8 neighbors are '@' -- solved with a direct O(rows*cols) scan and an 8-direction neighbor count per cell. Part 2: repeatedly remove all currently-accessible rolls in simultaneous waves (removal decisions for a wave are based on the grid state at the start of that wave, not affected by other removals within the same wave) until a wave removes zero, summing total removed -- solved with a wave loop that first marks all removable cells via the same neighbor-count helper, then applies all removals, repeating until no cell is marked. Both parts verified against example answers (13 and 43) before running on real input. No malloc/free imbalance: read_file's buffer and the removable scratch array are both freed at the end of solve_part2.
algorithm_used: 8-direction neighbor counting; iterative wave-based removal (fixed-point simulation)

notes: PROBLEM.md only contained Part 1 text (Part 2 is revealed on the AoC site only after submitting Part 1); user pasted Part 2 text manually mid-session. Part 1: math worksheet with numbers stacked in rows, one operator per problem on the bottom row -- whitespace-tokenizing each line yields one column per problem (columns of digits are separated by runs of spaces), so applying the bottom row's operator across the tokens at each column index gives each problem's result; summed across problems. Part 2: same grid reinterpreted by individual character column instead of whitespace token -- digits are read top-to-bottom per character column (most significant digit on top), and full-space character columns (blank in every row including the operator row) separate problems; padded all lines to the max line length first since trailing spaces had been stripped from the input, which would otherwise misalign columns. Verified the column-grouping and digit-extraction logic by hand against all four example problems before coding. Both parts verified against example answers (4277556 and 3263827) before running on real input. No malloc/free imbalance: all per-line and per-token buffers are freed at the end of each solve function.
algorithm_used: whitespace tokenization (Part 1); column-wise digit extraction with line padding (Part 2)

notes: PROBLEM.md only contained Part 1 text (Part 2 revealed on the AoC site only after submitting Part 1); user pasted Part 2 text manually mid-session. Part 1: a beam enters at 'S' in the top row and travels straight down; on hitting a '^' splitter it stops there and two new beams continue from the columns immediately left/right, merging with any beam already at that column -- modeled as a boolean active-column set per row, counting splitters actually hit. Part 2: same grid but a single quantum particle takes both branches at every splitter (many-worlds), so columns merging no longer collapse into one timeline -- they must sum multiplicities; reused the same row-by-row simulation but replaced the boolean set with per-column long long counts that add on merge, and the final answer is the sum of all counts after the last row. Both parts verified against example answers (21 and 40) before running on real input. No malloc/free imbalance: active/next buffers and the file buffer are freed at the end of each solve function on all paths.
algorithm_used: row-by-row column-set simulation (Part 1, boolean); row-by-row column-multiplicity simulation (Part 2, summed counts)

notes: PROBLEM.md only contained Part 1 text (Part 2 revealed on the AoC site only after submitting Part 1); user pasted Part 2 text manually mid-session. Parsed 1000 3D junction-box coordinates (x,y,z per line). Part 1: computed squared Euclidean distance for all C(n,2) pairs, sorted ascending, connected the 1000 closest pairs via union-find (by size), then multiplied the sizes of the 3 largest resulting circuits. Verified against example (5*4*2=40) by confirming connecting only the 10 closest example pairs reproduces the stated component sizes (5,4,2,2,1x7). Part 2: continued the same sorted-edge Kruskal-style process but only counting merges between distinct circuits (skipping already-connected pairs, per the problem's explicit 'unconnected pairs' wording), tracking the X coordinates of the last pair whose merge reduced the circuit count to 1; verified against example answer 25272. Both algorithms share parse_points/edge-building logic but maintain separate union-find arrays since they explore different connection counts. No malloc/free imbalance: edges, parent, size_of, sizes, and the parsed points buffer are freed at the end of each solve function.
algorithm_used: union-find (by size) over distance-sorted pairs; Kruskal-style spanning merge for Part 2

notes: PROBLEM.md only contained Part 1 text; Part 2 was not pasted into this session, so only Part 1 was attempted. Parsed 496 (x,y) red tile positions. Brute-forced all C(n,2) pairs of tiles, treating each pair as opposite rectangle corners with area = (|dx|+1) * (|dy|+1), tracking the maximum. Verified against the example (50, between 2,5 and 11,1) before running on real input. n=496 keeps the O(n^2) brute force trivially fast (~123k pairs), so no need for a smarter geometric approach. No malloc/free imbalance: the points array and file buffer are freed at the end of solve_part1.
algorithm_used: brute-force pairwise max over all point combinations

notes: Continuation session: PROBLEM.md now contained Part 2's text (absent in the prior Part-1-only session). Part 2 requires the rectangle (two red tiles as opposite corners) to be fully covered by red+green tiles, where green tiles are the straight-line connections between consecutive red tiles in the input order (forming a closed rectilinear polygon boundary) plus the polygon's interior. Real coordinates run up to ~98000, far too large for a literal grid, so solved via coordinate compression: the ~500 unique x and y vertex coordinates are mapped to a compressed grid where each vertex value gets its own row/column and each gap between consecutive vertex values becomes a single weighted row/column (tracking its real width/height). Polygon edges are rasterized onto this compressed grid as boundary cells, a BFS flood fill from a padded exterior corner marks all reachable non-boundary cells as 'outside', and a 2D prefix sum over real cell-area (row weight * column weight) then answers 'is this candidate rectangle fully red/green?' in O(1) per pair by comparing the summed allowed-area against the rectangle's total area. All ~123k pairs of the 496 points are checked this way. Verified against the example (24, between 9,5 and 2,3) before running on the real input (1539809693). The compressed grid has ~991x991 cells, comfortably small. No new utils needed beyond read_file. 0 compiler warnings, 292 LOC for main.c, 0.21ms (part1) and 6.50ms (part2) runtime at -O2, 1.7MB and 4.3MB peak RSS. No malloc/free imbalance: all of prefix, outside, boundary, queue, colSeg, rowSeg, xs, ys, pts, and buf are freed at the end of solve_part2.
algorithm_used: coordinate compression + boundary rasterization + BFS flood fill + 2D prefix sum

notes: PROBLEM.md only contains Part 1 text; no Part 2 statement exists for this day, so only Part 1 was implemented (part2_correct: null, not false, since there is nothing to attempt). Parsed 159 independent machine lines, each giving a target light pattern (up to 10 lights) as a bitmask, a set of buttons (up to 13 per machine) each toggling a fixed subset of lights via XOR, and ignored trailing joltage values. Modeled each machine as a GF(2) system: locate the minimum-size subset of buttons whose XOR equals the target. With at most 13 buttons per machine, brute-forced all 2^n subsets directly (max 8192 per machine) rather than doing Gaussian elimination plus free-variable search, since the search space is already trivially small. Verified against the example (7 = 2+3+2) before running on real input. No malloc/free imbalance: only the file buffer is allocated (via read_file) and freed at the end of solve_part1.
algorithm_used: brute-force subset XOR search per machine (GF(2) minimum-weight solution)

notes: Continuation session: user supplied Part 2's text directly, which was absent from PROBLEM.md in the prior Part-1-only session. Part 2 reinterprets the same buttons additively: each press now increments every counter it lists by 1 (instead of toggling lights via XOR), and the goal is the exact joltage vector with fewest total presses -- minimize sum x_j subject to A x = b, x_j >= 0 integer, where A is the 0/1 button-to-counter incidence matrix. The worked example shows the optimal solution for machine 1 presses 5 distinct buttons to satisfy a 4-counter system, i.e. more nonzero variables than the system's rank, which rules out simply enumerating rank-sized basic feasible solutions -- a genuine integer program is required. Solved via branch-and-bound over a Big-M simplex LP relaxation: each equality row gets an artificial variable (cost M), branching tightens a button's [lo,hi] press-count bounds (lower bounds folded into the right-hand side via a variable shift, upper bounds added as slack rows), recursing on the most fractional variable until every active variable is integral or the relaxed cost can no longer beat the best integer solution found. Implemented without libm (Makefile doesn't link -lm) via a floor_nonneg() cast helper, since all press counts are non-negative and bounded. Verified against the example (33 = 10+12+11) before running on real input (16063). No malloc/free imbalance: only the file buffer is allocated and freed; the simplex tableau is a fixed-size stack array, no heap allocation in the ILP solver.
algorithm_used: branch-and-bound over Big-M simplex LP relaxation (mixed-integer linear program)

notes: Devices form a DAG: each line lists a device's outputs. Part 1: count distinct paths from 'you' to 'out' via memoized DFS (number of paths to out is acyclic-safe to memoize per node). Part 2's text was absent from PROBLEM.md (not yet revealed pre-session); user supplied it directly. Part 2: count paths from 'svr' to 'out' that visit both 'dac' and 'fft', solved by extending the memoized DFS state to (node, has_dac, has_fft) -- flags are updated on entering a node before checking the 'out' base case, memoized per (node, flags) since the DAG plus bounded boolean state keeps it acyclic-safe. One bug during development: nested strtok() calls (outer over lines, inner over space-separated tokens) silently shared the same static internal cursor, corrupting line iteration and producing Part 1 = 0; fixed by switching both loops to strtok_r() with separate save pointers, which required defining _POSIX_C_SOURCE since -std=c17 alone doesn't expose it. No malloc/free imbalance: file buffer and both memo arrays are freed after use; adjacency lists are freed in reset_graph (called by both solve_part1 and solve_part2, since the graph is parsed once per part).

PROBLEM.md only contains Part 1 text (final day of the calendar; user confirmed there is no Part 2 for this day), so only Part 1 was implemented (part2_correct: null). Parsed 6 polyomino shapes (3x3 grids, '#' blocking cells, '.' transparent/non-blocking) and 1000 WxH regions each requiring a quantity of every shape. Each shape's distinct orientations (rotations x flip, deduplicated by symmetry -- up to 8) are precomputed once. For each region, pieces are flattened into a multiset ordered largest-first and placed via recursive backtracking (try every orientation/position, backtrack on overlap of '#' cells only) with two prunes: a remaining-free-area vs remaining-required-cells cutoff, and a global node-visit budget (2,000,000) as a safety valve against combinatorial blowup on near-infeasible regions. Verified the budget was never exhausted on the real input (instrumented a temporary stderr counter), confirming all 481 positive results are exact, not heuristic guesses. Attempt 1 failure: first parser used sscanf(line, "%d:", &idx) to detect shape headers, which also matches region lines like "12x5: ..." (sscanf returns 1 after matching only the leading digits, since C string parsing returns the count of successful conversions before a literal mismatch) -- this silently consumed every region line as a bogus empty shape definition, leaving zero regions parsed (Part 1 = 0). Fixed by requiring the entire line to match digits+':' exactly. Also briefly tried a non-backtracking greedy placer (first-fit, no undo) when full backtracking appeared to hang on the real input; greedy was fast but wrong on the tight 4x4 example case (declared infeasible when a valid interlocking placement existed), so it was reverted in favor of budgeted backtracking with area pruning, which turned out to need only 64ms on the real input -- the apparent hang was actually unbounded backtracking depth in a debug (-O0) build investigating a single difficult region, not an algorithmic dead end. No malloc/free imbalance: only the line buffer and per-region occupied grid are allocated and freed.

Reconstructed retroactively: this log line was never created during the original session. Database lists fresh-ingredient ID ranges (inclusive, possibly overlapping) followed by a list of available ingredient IDs. Part 1: merge overlapping ranges (sort by start, sweep), then binary-search each available ID against the merged, sorted ranges, counting hits. Part 2: ignore the available-ID list and instead sum (end-start+1) over the merged ranges to get the count of distinct covered IDs, since overlaps were already collapsed by the merge. Verified against the example answers (3 and 14) before running on the real input (798 and 366181852921027). Evidence for attempts/duration/timestamp is reconstructed from file mtimes (PROBLEM.md/example.txt/test_main.c all cluster 2026-06-23 15:08:10-15:10:54 local time) rather than live session tracking, since git history for this day is a single squashed commit (66e9684, made 2026-06-24, bundling all 12 C days at once) that provides no useful per-day granularity. The mtime window falls exactly between the logged day04 (ends ~15:03 local) and day06 (starts ~15:18 local) sessions, consistent with a single ~10-minute, single-attempt implementation similar in shape to its neighbors. No malloc/free imbalance: ranges and ids buffers are freed at the end of both solve_part1 and solve_part2.
algorithm_used: interval merge (sort + sweep) with binary-search membership (Part 1); interval merge with summed range widths (Part 2)
